item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I always enjoy a good article where the object isn't actively anomalous, but rather is indicative something for more threatening than the object itself. This is marvelously done.

item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Image could not be sourced reliably and is assumed not compliant with CC 3.0. As such, it has since been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: From what I've seen of your work, you really know how to spin a seemingly random anomaly into an interesting, compelling narrative, and this one is definitely no exception. The passive way this tells a weird but oddly intriguing story about the lineage of Montenegro's prime minister is just something I have to applaud. Excellently done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Concerning Đukanović’s reappearance in the future, why is everyone saying “zombie president”? My first thought was “time travel”.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think I saw another article posted back in 2014 where the president in 2018 was ██████ █████ (6,5).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this article a lot because it feels genuinely inexplicable and unsolved. No exposition, just a mystery.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You know, this is sort of creepy. I don't know why I feel that. Maybe it has something todo with the fact that I read this before I go to bed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this SCP overall, but I find the censorship of its location rather unnecessary. A quick Google reveals the "Garden of Love Pet Memorial Park" is located in Micanopy, Florida. Perhaps it could be explicitly stated to be in Micanopy or have the park's full name redacted instead?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know how you did it, but you managed to predict the exact number of letters in the first and last names of the current PM of Montenegro back in 2014. Bravo.

item.title: SCP-2072, and why I downvoted it.
item.content:encoded:
Simply put:
{{**//What.//**}}

Generally, the "what factor" on an SCP object is a very good thing, especially if it's done correctly. This is not one of those cases. The "what factor" of this object is just way to much to be healthy, leaving me trying not to laugh in confusion. I understood what was happening with the object as I read it, but, it still just killed my suspension of disbelief entirely. All and all, it's just an iffy concept that could've totally be cool, but fell flat.

-1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now, the prime minister of Montenegro has been changed to Duško Marković. Is it need edit?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I hate that my first two comments after joining the site are going to essentially express the same sentiment, but this one really doesn't do anything for me. As many people mentioned already, there's zero reason why the Foundation would dispatch an MTF to investigate human bodies in a pet cemetery, because that in and of itself is not anomalous.

The circumstances surrounding the bodies are weird, but that's about it. The Godhead buzzword seems tacked on and doesn't do anything for the article other than add another shade of "this is bizarre".

This one just feels a little sloppy and lacking any real impact, especially when compared to your other creations. Am I missing something?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Silicon powder makes me think silicon valley and tech. Quick google says silicon powder is fundamental in semi-conductors….

Maybe that prime minister was a computer?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The last one made me laugh.
It has to be a zombie-politician.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Random, cyclic, strange… Not world-ending or creepy, but unsettling.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: … Why would the coffin for "Godhead" be filled with pure silicon powder? If it were filled with something more typically religious in nature, like holy water, I could understand. But pure silicon powder??? That's… kinda out of left field. And it makes me very curious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this entry! This is a perfect example of a case where NOT knowing the specifics and leaving things to imagination enhances the experience, rather than making it feel cheap or like an author cop-out for not being able to think of anything.

Also, I like that it's legitimately got some scary stuff going on without the tone being all about that. All in all, very well done.

Heartily upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think it's about Milo Đukanović. He died before some of the later corpses were born, jet he apparently becomes a prime minister again after them. So, he might, at some point, shift to the future (and then back again before he dies).

Or he'll be an undead president.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really want to like this, but one thing feels out of place

"Foundation Local Mobile Task Force 352-Lamedh (“Stump Knockers”) seized control of the area to investigate"

why on earth are the foundation the first response in a situation like this? it makes no sense. If human bodies are found in a pet cemetary i'd think the police would be involved first, and then the foundation would catch them when they find that the bodies have the same genetics/oddities. Its possible that it happened that way and you shortened it, but it doesnt read that way, so im withholding an upvote till i get confirmation or a good explanation for why they'd be watching it that closely

item.title: Re: Wait a minute... .
item.content:encoded:
It's not even like pet cemetary >.< pet cemetary is the corpses buried there being brought back to life by demons

EDIT: woops that was last august not yesterday XD

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is honestly really interesting. Milo taking over at the end again just… Makes it creepier to me, to be honest. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually liked this as much as I did not like your 001 proposal. Enthusiastically translated, reviews on our site are positive as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Love it. It has enough weird in it to be interesting, but also enough story for it not to feel like some random event. The repetition of Milo Đukanović was a nice touch.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fantastic SCP. Do you have an idea of where this is going, or is it just sort of up in the air?

Also, do you plan to update this for the future, or should we pretend it's an alternate universe?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fantastic. This is what SCPs should be like.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Creepy to strange to unsettling to severely unsettling to funny. Fantastic work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love it,

just enough creep factor and "what?" moments to do it for me.
long live godhead the eternal

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fair enough. Still a huge fan of this one, and excited to see more from this series you're doing. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I read this a while ago, and there's nothing about the article I wouldn't love.
I love the implication for timeline consistency that the repeated Djukanovic entries have, as well as the fact that this is set in former Jugoslavia. (For one, the Church of Nod in the Tiberium Wars setting emerged in Bosnia IIRC. For two the region has pretty awesome history, fairly underexploited in fiction, and for three, Slavic brothers and sisters, I guess x3.)
Enthusiastic upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upon the discovery of human remains beneath the northeast section of headstones in 1977, Foundation Local Mobile Task Force 352-Lamedh (“Stump Knockers”) seized control of the area to investigate.

Just finding human corpses in a pet cemetery is enough to get the Foundation to investigate?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is an amazingly concise SCP. This is the stuff I aspire to create… You are very good. Take my upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you so much for your feedback! I totally understand where you're coming from re: the investigation. But the idea of local mobile task forces deeply embedded in local law enforcement and events factors heavily into my personal foundation universe and why I wrote the object in the first place. I'm gonna be stubborn about it even though your points are totally valid. c:

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
With Montenegrin graves in Micanopy, a small village that exists simultaneously in Romania and just outside Ocala, and the constant discovery of slips of paper containing the complete bank records of citizens of Croatia floating through the sky in rural Walton County, Foundation researchers theorize there is an extranormal connection between North Florida and the Balkans. The significance of this is not yet clear.1

Footnotes
1. Okay, actual reason: Montenegro has a short history, which allows the graveyard to be complete and get to the fun future stuff faster.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Love it; simple, brief, weird. A neat idea executed elegantly. Upvoted.

Out of curiosity, why Montenegro?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Kalinin totally said what I came here to say. I am so incredibly impressed at the creativity and economy of words that is on display here. This is an epic tale of politics, society, and science fiction told through a table. One table! It's this kind of thing that makes this site for me one of the most interesting things happening in fiction writing today. +1

That being said, I too find it implausible that the Foundation would investigate human remains being found in a pet cemetery. Everyone would just assume it was some killer's dump site. It would make a lot more sense to say that the Foundation stepped in when the cops ID'd the body as the still-living Prime Minister. Then they could amnestic the cops and secure the site. I hope you'll consider it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is really good. Like on-my-top-ten good. It tells so much story so cleverly, without obvious tricks, and has tons of WTF and a nice dash of creepy. Each new grave felt fresh and interesting, and a nice long run of surprises. The empty grave and Milo's return got me especially.

item.title: Re: Wait a minute... .
item.content:encoded:
I've actually never read that!

I ate paninis in a pet cemetery in rural North Florida with a friend last week. The atmosphere was wonderfully creepy, so I left with this idea and about 50 mosquito bites.

item.title: Wait a minute... .
item.content:encoded: Is this…. What I think it is? Did you get this from Pet Sematary? I will love you for ever if it is. Stephen is just… so, like, AMAZING.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I started that log knowing things had to get weird. There was no way it wouldn't. I got to the cyborg and was like "A-ha! Here we go." Then came the Godhead, which amused me. Then the empty coffin gave me a "…huh."

Then you finally catch me off guard by bringing back Milo. Curse you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This has got just the right amount of creep and mystery. Love it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Let's be real here: If a task force is monitoring all of the weird news in Florida they are all probably dying of sleep deprivation and exhaustion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
[beware: headcanon ahead]

LMTF 352 is responsible for investigating weird news stories they can get to in an eleven-county area of North Florida. They're not omnipresent, but this was only a few miles from their Gainesville headquarters and Wallace was tired of losing at ping pong when the Sun story came down the wire. Lucky catch, but they probably would've got to it after the Sheriff found out about the "genetically identical" thing.

And the Foundation are packrats, and have a lot of weird/anomalous materials with DNA in them. They don't keep everyone's genetic information, but the Foundation (and its giant, lumbering, well-funded bureaucracy) have made it a priority since the late 80's to keep as much genetic info as possible, starting with world leaders. Who wouldn't want to know that the flesh lump they found bouncing around a racquetball court in Amsterdam appeared to come from former Mexican president Vincente Fox?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a great example of how to do story in the SCP format. The log is pleasingly weird and provides enough storytelling to generate interest in the reader, without resorting to a Dr. Author figure explaining things directly or a convenient note mysteriously left behind. I enjoyed how the entries in the list progressed, and I really liked the idea that somewhere out there, the future prime minister of Montenegro is blissfully unaware that a corpse identical to them is buried in a pet cemetery in Florida.

That all said, I would enjoy this even more if the background stuff was pared down a bit. I found the idea of the Foundation intervening in a situation just because someone decided to bury humans in a pet cemetery a little belief-stretching, and the notion that everyone's genetic information is on file at the Foundation even more so. For me, that stuff didn't really add much to the article.

On balance, though, this was good. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is there a reason for tagging this with temporal? Is it directly affecting how time passes, or is it simple predicting it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Corrected the dates. Thank you very much for the feedback!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Tale. Write it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I rather like this one. It makes me interested to see that they extrapolate from the information that appears to them, to use the gravestones predictively. The inclusion of the current prime minister at the end maybe sends it a little too far for my suspension of disbelief, but it has very interesting implications nonetheless.

Looks like the DOB for the first iteration of Milo Đukanović is different to that of subsequent ones, was that deliberate? Also, the entry for Filip Vujanović marks his birthdate as the same as Đukanović. Looks like those might have been swapped for whatever reason.

Overall though, I like this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Convoluted, yeah, but wouldn't you want to know the stories of the SCP Foundation team responsible for collecting genetic material from every world leader for the Foundation Genetics Database?

Hm. That gives me an idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While I have to admit the discovery and initial containment of this thing feels a bit convoluted to me, the severe WHAT factor grants it an upvote.

Therefore, upvote. Good job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fixed the metric issue. Three years on the site and I still manage to imperial everything up, go figure.

The Foundation doesn't know what the hell the last entry's about, either. They've had people monitoring Đukanović every day for the last twenty-plus years for any sign of temporally anomalous qualities, and have thus far found seemingly no explanation for why his corpse shows up again in the far future.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this one, but I have a few minor problems. In the notes section of the "godhead" entries you have 5" of silicon powder, and that should be metric. I also don't really get what you where trying to imply with the last entry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting and well-written.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you! I've fixed the numbering issues identified. I very much appreciate the feedback.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is interesting, but I think you have some labeling issues. The Containment description seemed like you might have forgotten a -XX and the graph goes -09,-10,-11,-12,-10 (which you then label as genetically identical to -10) the previous genetically identical corpses all got their own numbers.

If this labeling issue is fixed, you have my +1.
